title: Assessment: Control State, Isolation, and Cleanup

questions.question: Why does the course treat isolation as a first-order TDD concern?

options.A: Because pytest requires every test to use the same fixture scope
options.B: Because passing tests are only trustworthy when each test controls its own environment
options.C: Because integration tests should never touch state
options.D: Because cleanup only matters in unittest

questions.explanation: The course argues that leaking state corrupts the signal that red-green-refactor depends on.

questions.question: What is usually the safest default fixture scope when strong isolation matters most?
questions.explanation: Function scope minimizes hidden sharing and gives the strongest default independence.

questions.question: What is the main value of wrapping a database test in an outer transaction that gets rolled back?

options.A: It makes commits impossible inside application code
options.B: It preserves realistic persistence behavior while still resetting state afterward
options.C: It replaces the need for all unit tests
options.D: It lets async tests run concurrently

questions.explanation: The pattern keeps real session behavior while guaranteeing cleanup for the next test.

questions.question: Explain why async loop scope and teardown policy affect TDD quality rather than just framework mechanics.

required_points: Loop scope changes whether async tests leak hidden state or lifecycle assumptions
Teardown policy affects whether tests remain deterministic
The red-green-refactor loop depends on believable, isolated feedback
answer_key.full_credit: Connects runtime lifecycle control directly to trustworthy design feedback
answer_key.partial_credit: Mentions async complexity but not its effect on feedback quality

questions.prompt: Design a testing strategy for a small async service that stores records in a database. Use Module 2 to explain how you would control fixture scope, event-loop lifecycle, and database cleanup without turning the suite into either mock theater or persistent-state chaos.
questions.guidance: Strong answers combine narrow default sharing, explicit loop judgment, and transactional cleanup or equivalent lifecycle control.

rubric.A: Builds a coherent isolation strategy across async runtime and persistence with clear tradeoff reasoning
rubric.B: Handles one boundary well but leaves another under-specified
rubric.C: Relies mostly on vague advice about writing more tests or using mocks
